title: Deploying to Vercel
intro: It's deployment day. It's like moving into a new apartment — everything worked at your old place, but the new environment has its own quirks. You connect your repo to Vercel, hit deploy, and... the build fails. Welcome to the real world of deployment debugging.

teach.title: Deploying a Next.js App
teach.body: Vercel is built by the creators of Next.js, making it the smoothest deployment option. You connect your GitHub repo, Vercel detects it's a Next.js app, and builds it automatically. But 'automatic' doesn't mean 'no issues' — missing env vars, build errors, and type errors are common first-deploy problems.

teach.title: Deploying Is Like Moving Into a New Apartment
teach.body: Everything worked in your old place, but in the new apartment the outlets are in different spots and the water pressure is different. Deploying to Vercel is similar — your code is the same, but the environment is new, and small differences (missing env vars, case-sensitive file systems) can trip you up.

teach.title: Common First-Deploy Errors
teach.code:
Error: NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL is not defined
→ You forgot to add env vars in Vercel dashboard

Type error: Property 'title' does not exist on type 'never'
→ TypeScript is stricter in production builds

Error: Module not found: Can't resolve './components/TaskList'
→ Case sensitivity: taskList.tsx vs TaskList.tsx (Linux is case-sensitive)
teach.annotation: Most first-deploy failures are environment or build configuration issues, not code bugs.

teach.body: Every successful deploy puts your product in front of real users. Mastering Vercel deployments means you can go from idea to live app in hours, not weeks — and speed to market is how indie builders make money.
Always run the production build locally (npm run build) before deploying. It catches most build errors that won't appear in development mode.

start.narrative: You push your task manager to GitHub and connect the repo to Vercel. The build starts and immediately fails with: 'Error: NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL is not defined.' What do you do?

choices.text: Hardcode the Supabase URL directly in the code so the build doesn't need env vars
Add the environment variables in the Vercel project settings dashboard, then redeploy
Push your .env file to the repo so Vercel can read it

hardcode.narrative: The build passes, but now your Supabase URL is visible in the public source code. A teammate flags it on review and asks you to back it out. What do you do?

choices.text: Revert the hardcode, add the env vars in Vercel settings, and redeploy properly
Argue it's fine since the URL is technically public anyway, and leave the hardcode in place

add-env-vars.narrative: You add the env vars in Vercel settings. The build passes. But the app shows a blank page. The browser console reveals: 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined.' What's your next step?

choices.text: Check Vercel's deployment logs and the browser console to find the exact failing component
Run npm run build locally to reproduce the issue in a local production build

push-env.narrative: You accidentally push your .env file with your real Supabase service role key. Within minutes, a bot scraping GitHub finds it. You rotate the key, scrub the file from history, and add .env to .gitignore. The build still fails because Vercel never had the env vars in the first place.

choices.text: Add the rotated env vars to Vercel project settings and redeploy
Delete the Vercel project entirely and start over from the dashboard

second-deploy-bug.narrative: The build is green and the env vars are wired up. But before promoting to production, you want to verify the deploy. Vercel automatically gave you a preview URL on the latest commit. How do you use it?

choices.text: Open the preview URL, sign in as a test user, create and complete a task, and confirm the data lands in Supabase
Skip the preview — the build succeeded, that's good enough to push live
Promote the preview to production immediately and test on the production URL afterward

domain-step.narrative: Preview looks good. Time to give the deploy a real address. You own taskmanager.app. How do you wire it up?

choices.text: Add taskmanager.app in Vercel's Domains settings and follow the DNS records it generates
Point a wildcard CNAME at vercel-dns.com without configuring the domain in the dashboard
Skip the custom domain — share the .vercel.app URL with users for now

monitoring-step.narrative: The app is live on your custom domain. Final question: how will you know if the next deploy breaks something for users?

choices.text: Enable Vercel's runtime logs and integrate an error tracker (Sentry) so failed requests page you
Open the app once a day and click around to make sure it still works
If the build is green, the app is fine — no extra monitoring needed

excellent-end.narrative: Your task manager is live on its own domain, the env vars are properly configured in Vercel, you verified the deploy on a preview URL before promoting, and runtime errors get reported the moment they happen. This is a deploy you can trust.
excellent-end.feedback: This is the full Vercel workflow: env vars in the dashboard, preview deploys for verification, custom domain via Vercel's DNS, and an error tracker for the things that only break in production. Future deploys are a one-click affair because the foundation is right.

mediocre-end.narrative: Your app is live and you know how to find issues — but only when you happen to look. The first time a deploy ships a regression at 11pm, your users will hit the bug for hours before you notice the next morning.
mediocre-end.feedback: Manual checks scale until they don't. An error tracker takes 15 minutes to wire up and surfaces problems you wouldn't catch on a click-around. Add it before your user count outgrows your eyeballs.

weak-end.narrative: Your app is live but you have no idea what users are actually experiencing. A bad merge ships a broken sign-up flow; you find out a week later from a support email asking why nobody can register.
weak-end.feedback: A green build only proves the code compiled, not that it works for real users. Without runtime logs and error tracking, you're flying blind. Treat observability as part of the deploy itself, not an afterthought.
